sources.title: Grade 5 - Term 3: An ancient African society: Egypt
Way of life in ancient Egypt
Early African Civilisations: Ancient Egypt, Nubia and Swahili
Glossary: An ancient African society: Egypt
Bibliography: An ancient African society: Egypt
An ancient African society - Egypt Activity
Archaeologists announce they have found treasures in the tomb of Tutankhamen

slides.title: An Ancient African Society: Egypt
slides.lead: A journey up the Nile to meet the pharaohs, pyramids and picture-writing of one of Africa's oldest civilisations.

media.alt: Three golden pyramids and a bright sun rising over the desert beside a blue river.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='250' cy='50' r='26' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect y='120' width='320' height='60' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='24' width='30' height='180' fill='#4a86b5' opacity='0.85'/><path d='M120 120 L160 52 L200 120 Z' fill='#d8b45a'/><path d='M70 120 L100 74 L130 120 Z' fill='#c8a24a'/><path d='M190 120 L215 82 L240 120 Z' fill='#c8a24a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Ask learners what they already know about ancient Egypt - pyramids, mummies, pharaohs. Explain that Egypt is in Africa, in the north-east, and that this was one of the world's earliest civilisations, going back more than 5000 years. A 'civilisation' means a large, organised society with towns, government, writing and shared beliefs.

slides.title: An African civilisation, long ago
slides.lead: Ancient Egypt grew in north-east Africa more than 5000 years ago.

blocks.text: Egypt is a country in the north-east of **Africa**. Thousands of years ago, one of the world's first great civilisations grew here. It lasted for a very long time because Egypt had natural borders - deserts, seas and cliffs - that made it hard for enemies to invade.

items: Egypt is part of the African continent, next to the Red Sea and the Mediterranean Sea.
The civilisation grew and changed over thousands of years.
We learn about it from ruins, tombs, paintings and ancient writing.

slides.notes: Reinforce the key CAPS idea: Egypt is an African society, not separate from Africa. Point out on a map that it is on our own continent. Explain that historians study the past using evidence - here that evidence is buildings, tombs, wall paintings and writing left behind. [SME] Confirm the map/location wording suits your class atlas.

slides.title: The Nile River
slides.lead: The ancient Greeks said that 'Egypt was the gift of the Nile'.

blocks.text: The **Nile** is the biggest river in Africa. It flows from the south to the north and empties into the Mediterranean Sea. Most of Egypt is hot, dry desert, so almost everyone lived close to the river.

Every summer the Nile **flooded**. When the water went down again, it left behind rich, dark soil called *alluvial soil*. This soil was perfect for growing crops, so the same fields could be farmed year after year.

items: The Nile gave people water to drink and to water their crops.
It was a busy road for boats carrying people and goods.
The yearly flood made the soil fertile for farming.

media.alt: A blue river running through green farmland with yellow desert on both sides.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#e8d9a8'/><rect x='104' width='32' height='160' fill='#4a86b5'/><rect x='80' width='24' height='160' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='136' width='24' height='160' fill='#7a8b5a'/><circle cx='40' cy='36' r='16' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the heart of the topic: geography shaped everything. Explain the flood cycle simply - water covers the fields, then drains away leaving fresh mud that makes plants grow well. Without the Nile, Egypt would be all desert. Ask: why did nearly everyone live close to the river?

slides.title: Living and working by the river
slides.lead: The river gave people food, homes and work all year round.

blocks.text: Long ago the first people here were hunter-gatherers who moved around to find food. Over time they settled down and became **farmers**, building homes and villages along the banks of the Nile.

items: Farmers grew wheat and barley to make bread, and vegetables for food.
They kept animals such as cattle, goats and ducks.
People used the river reed *papyrus* to make boats, mats and an early kind of paper.
Homes were built from mud bricks that dried hard in the sun.

media.alt: Tall papyrus reeds growing beside water with a small reed boat.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='220' height='40' fill='#4a86b5'/><g stroke='#3f6b45' stroke-width='4' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='120' x2='40' y2='44'/><line x1='58' y1='120' x2='58' y2='54'/><line x1='76' y1='120' x2='76' y2='40'/></g><g fill='#7a8b5a'><circle cx='40' cy='40' r='9'/><circle cx='58' cy='50' r='9'/><circle cx='76' cy='36' r='9'/></g><path d='M120 132 q30 -18 60 0 q-30 10 -60 0 Z' fill='#c8a24a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Bring daily life alive. Papyrus is a good link word - it grew in the river and was used to make an early paper, which is where our word 'paper' comes from. Mud-brick houses connect to what learners may know about building materials. Emphasise how much daily life depended on the river and its floods.

slides.title: Upper and Lower Egypt join together
slides.lead: Two parts of Egypt were united under one ruler.

blocks.text: Because the Nile flows from south to north, Egypt was divided into two lands. The southern part was called **Upper Egypt** and the northern part, near the sea, was called **Lower Egypt**. This may feel upside-down, but 'upper' means higher up the river, closer to where it begins.
Long ago these two lands were joined into one kingdom ruled by a single king. This began the long line of Egyptian kings we call **pharaohs**.

items: Upper Egypt was in the south, Lower Egypt in the north.
They were united into one country under one ruler.
The king wore a special crown showing he ruled both lands.

media.alt: A simple map showing Upper Egypt in the south and Lower Egypt in the north joined by the Nile.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 170' role='img'><rect width='200' height='170' fill='#e8d9a8'/><rect x='88' y='10' width='24' height='150' fill='#4a86b5'/><rect x='60' y='10' width='80' height='70' fill='#a8bcae' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='60' y='92' width='80' height='68' fill='#c8b89a' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='84' y='6' width='32' height='10' fill='#4a86b5'/></svg>

slides.notes: Clear up the common confusion: 'Upper' Egypt is in the south because it is higher up the river's course. Use the river-flow direction to explain it. The uniting of the two lands is the moment Egypt became one kingdom. [SME] Historians date this to roughly 3000 BCE; keep the wording as 'long ago' unless you want to introduce the date.

slides.title: The pharaoh: king and god
slides.lead: The pharaoh was the most powerful person in all of Egypt.

blocks.text: The king of Egypt was called a **pharaoh**. The word comes from the ancient Egyptian words *per-aa*, which mean 'great house'. The pharaoh was the leader of the government and the leader of the religion at the same time.

items: People believed the pharaoh was a god in the shape of a person.
He owned the land and made the laws.
He led the army and was in charge of building temples and pyramids.
When a pharaoh died, his son usually became the next pharaoh.

media.alt: The head of a pharaoh wearing the tall striped Egyptian crown.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 160 170' role='img'><rect width='160' height='170' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M56 60 q24 -40 48 0 v70 h-48 Z' fill='#c8a24a'/><g stroke='#4a86b5' stroke-width='6'><line x1='60' y1='70' x2='60' y2='128'/><line x1='72' y1='64' x2='72' y2='128'/><line x1='88' y1='64' x2='88' y2='128'/><line x1='100' y1='70' x2='100' y2='128'/></g><ellipse cx='80' cy='108' rx='22' ry='26' fill='#d8a878'/><circle cx='72' cy='104' r='3' fill='#3a2a1a'/><circle cx='88' cy='104' r='3' fill='#3a2a1a'/><path d='M74 118 q6 6 12 0' stroke='#3a2a1a' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/></svg>

slides.notes: The pharaoh combined king, government leader and religious leader. This joining of ruler and god gave the pharaoh huge power. 'Great house' (per-aa) first meant the royal palace and later the ruler himself. [SME] Some sources also say the Hebrews used the word 'pharaoh'; keep the explanation simple at Grade 5 and stick to the 'great house' meaning.

slides.title: The shape of Egyptian society
slides.lead: Egyptian society was like a pyramid: a few powerful people at the top, many workers at the bottom.

blocks.heading: The pharaoh
blocks.text: At the very top, the god-king who ruled over everyone and everything.

blocks.heading: Priests, scribes and officials
blocks.text: Educated people who ran the temples, kept the records and helped the pharaoh govern.

blocks.heading: Farmers, craftworkers and labourers
blocks.text: The many ordinary people who grew the food, made goods and built the great monuments.

slides.notes: Use the pyramid shape as a memory tool for the social order - it matches the buildings they are famous for. Scribes were important: they could read and write, so they kept records and had special jobs. Point out that most people were farmers and workers whose labour supported everyone above them.

slides.title: Gods and the afterlife
slides.lead: The ancient Egyptians believed in many gods and in life after death.

blocks.text: The Egyptians worshipped **many gods and goddesses**, who they believed controlled the sun, the river, the harvest and other parts of life. Powerful priests looked after the temples and the religious ceremonies.

They believed strongly in an **afterlife** - a life that continues after death. To prepare a body for the afterlife, they carefully preserved it as a **mummy**. This was expensive, so only the rich, and especially the pharaohs, could afford it.

items: Egyptians believed the body needed to be kept safe for the afterlife.
Bodies were made into mummies and placed in tombs with food and treasures.
Religion and the pharaoh's power were closely linked.

media.alt: A wrapped mummy lying inside a decorated coffin.

slides.notes: Handle mummies with curiosity and respect, not as something spooky - explain it was a serious religious practice to care for the dead. The belief in the afterlife is the reason for the pyramids and the treasures inside tombs, which connects to the next slides. Note that religion supported the pharaoh's authority.

slides.title: Pyramids and the Sphinx
slides.lead: The Egyptians built giant stone tombs that still stand today.

blocks.text: The Egyptians built huge stone **pyramids** as tombs for their pharaohs and queens. Inside were storage rooms, hidden passages and courtyards. The pharaoh's mummy and riches were placed inside for the afterlife.
Near the pyramids at Giza stands the **Sphinx**, a giant statue with the body of a lion and the head of a king. It has stood as a symbol of Egypt for thousands of years.

items: Pyramids were built by large teams of skilled craftworkers and labourers.
Much of the building happened during the flood season, when farmers could not work the fields.
The most famous pyramids are at Giza, near modern Cairo.

media.alt: Two large pyramids and the Sphinx statue in the desert.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#e8d9a8'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#d8c48a'/><path d='M60 120 L110 40 L160 120 Z' fill='#c8a24a'/><path d='M150 120 L190 62 L230 120 Z' fill='#b7935a'/><g fill='#c8a24a'><rect x='20' y='104' width='40' height='16'/><path d='M18 104 q16 -22 30 0 Z'/></g><circle cx='28' cy='100' r='2' fill='#3a2a1a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Marvel at the scale - these were built by hand, thousands of years ago, and are still standing. Explain that ordinary farmers helped build them during the flood, when their fields were underwater. The Sphinx links back to the pharaoh's power (a lion's strength, a king's head). Keep the tone one of wonder at African achievement.

slides.title: The tomb of Tutankhamen
slides.lead: In 1922 explorers found the treasure-filled tomb of a young pharaoh.

blocks.text: **Tutankhamen** was a pharaoh who became king as a boy and died young. For thousands of years his tomb lay hidden and untouched. In **1922** it was discovered, still full of golden treasures, a golden mask and everyday objects buried with him.

items: The tomb taught us a great deal about how pharaohs were buried.
It was filled with gold, jewellery, furniture and food for the afterlife.
Tutankhamen is now one of the most famous pharaohs in the world.

media.alt: A golden pharaoh burial mask with blue stripes.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 160 170' role='img'><rect width='160' height='170' fill='#2f2a3a'/><path d='M52 44 q28 -26 56 0 v40 q0 44 -28 62 q-28 -18 -28 -62 Z' fill='#e0b83a'/><path d='M52 44 q28 -26 56 0 v14 h-56 Z' fill='#4a86b5'/><g stroke='#4a86b5' stroke-width='4'><line x1='58' y1='58' x2='58' y2='96'/><line x1='102' y1='58' x2='102' y2='96'/></g><circle cx='70' cy='78' r='4' fill='#2f2a3a'/><circle cx='90' cy='78' r='4' fill='#2f2a3a'/><path d='M72 100 q8 6 16 0' stroke='#2f2a3a' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/><rect x='74' y='112' width='12' height='18' fill='#4a86b5'/></svg>

slides.notes: The 1922 discovery by archaeologists is exciting for learners - a real hidden treasure. It matters to historians because most royal tombs were robbed long ago, but Tutankhamen's was almost complete, so it is a rich source of evidence about beliefs and daily objects. [SME] The tomb was found by Howard Carter's team in 1922; add his name if you wish to name the archaeologist.

slides.title: Hieroglyphs: writing with pictures
slides.lead: The Egyptians invented a form of writing that used pictures and symbols.

blocks.text: As trade grew, the Egyptians needed to keep records of what they bought and sold. They developed a kind of picture-writing called **hieroglyphs**, where each little picture or symbol stood for a sound or an idea.

items: Hieroglyphs were carved on temple walls and written on papyrus.
Specially trained writers called *scribes* learned to read and write them.
This writing helped the Egyptians record their history, laws and stories.

media.alt: A row of Egyptian hieroglyph symbols: an eye, a bird, water lines and a foot.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 120' role='img'><rect width='240' height='120' fill='#e8d9b8'/><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='100' fill='none' stroke='#b7935a' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M30 60 q20 -18 40 0 q-20 18 -40 0 Z' fill='none' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='50' cy='60' r='6' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M92 46 q22 -6 34 8 l-10 4 q-8 12 -24 8 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/><g stroke='#4a86b5' stroke-width='3' fill='none'><path d='M150 52 q6 -6 12 0 q6 6 12 0'/><path d='M150 64 q6 -6 12 0 q6 6 12 0'/></g><path d='M200 44 v28 h16' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='4' fill='none'/></svg>

slides.notes: Link writing to trade and record-keeping - a practical need led to a great invention. Explain 'scribe' as a person whose special job was to write, and that reading and writing gave scribes an important place in society. If time allows, let learners invent a picture-symbol for their own name.

slides.title: Gifts from ancient Egypt
slides.lead: Ideas from ancient Egypt helped later peoples all over the world.

blocks.heading: Mathematics
blocks.text: The Egyptians created an early number system and used it to measure land and plan their buildings.

blocks.heading: Medicine
blocks.text: Egyptian doctors set broken bones, stitched wounds, treated illnesses with herbs and even worked on teeth.

blocks.heading: Writing and paper
blocks.text: Their picture-writing and papyrus 'paper' were among the earliest ways of keeping records.

slides.notes: Draw out continuity and change: ideas from long ago still matter today. Egyptians measured fields and worked out how to build straight, even pyramids using maths. Their knowledge of the body came partly from preparing mummies. Stress that these achievements came from an African society.

slides.title: Ancient Egypt through time

events.label: Hunter-gatherers settle and become farmers along the Nile
Upper and Lower Egypt are united under one pharaoh
Great pyramids and the Sphinx are built at Giza
Egypt is ruled by pharaohs, priests and scribes
The tomb of Tutankhamen is discovered, full of treasure

slides.notes: Use the timeline to review the whole lesson in order. Explain that 'BCE' dates are hard at Grade 5, so we use 'long ago' and 'about 5000 years ago'. Ask learners to retell the story of Egypt using the timeline as a guide. [SME] Adjust to precise BCE dates (unification c.3000 BCE, Giza pyramids c.2500 BCE) if your class uses them.

slides.title: What we have learned
slides.lead: Ancient Egypt was a great African civilisation shaped by the Nile, ruled by pharaohs and remembered for its pyramids and picture-writing.

blocks.text: The **Nile River** gave the Egyptians water, food and a way to travel. The **pharaoh** ruled as king and god, helped by priests and scribes. The Egyptians believed in **many gods** and in life after death, and they built **pyramids** as tombs. They invented **hieroglyph** writing and made discoveries in maths and medicine that still matter today.

blocks.question: Explain two ways the Nile River was important to the ancient Egyptians.
Who was the pharaoh, and why was he so powerful?
Name one thing the ancient Egyptians built or invented, and say why it was important.

slides.notes: Close by linking all the threads back to the Nile - geography made the civilisation possible. Use the three questions for written work or class discussion; the mark allocations follow CAPS Intermediate Phase style. Remind learners that this was an African civilisation, connecting the topic to the study of Africa's history.
